C:
Low free-to-paid for a note app is almost always an activation-and-habit problem wearing a pricing costume, not a pricing problem. People pay for a tool they can't imagine losing — so the lever is getting them dependent, then putting the paywall exactly where a dependent user naturally hits a wall. Before you touch price, find out which of those two things is actually broken.

Here's the sketch, in the order the work should happen.

**1. Split the funnel before changing anything.** Separate two cohorts: users who never activated (signed up, wrote a few notes, drifted off) versus users who clearly retained (come back weekly, dozens of notes) but still won't pay. The fix is opposite for each, and most teams optimize the wrong one. My strong prior: the bulk of your loss is in the first group — people who never got dependent enough for the upgrade question to even arise. Conversion of *retained* users is usually fine; you just have too few retained users.

**2. If the leak is activation, fix that first — it's upstream of everything.** A faster path to "this is now where I keep my notes": frictionless capture, a real first-note within 60 seconds, import from wherever their notes live today, and mobile + desktop sync working on day one. No amount of paywall tuning helps if people aren't forming the habit.

**3. Put the paywall on the dimension that compounds with dependence.** Free should be genuinely useful but capped on something a committed user inevitably outgrows. For note apps the candidates that convert well: number of devices/sync, search across your whole library, AI over your notes (summarize, ask-questions, auto-organize), version history, attachment storage, and sharing/export. The mistake is gating something people decide about on day one (when they don't care yet) instead of month two (when leaving would cost them their notes).

**4. Make the upgrade prompt contextual, not a settings page.** Trigger it at the moment of friction — "you've hit 3 devices," "search is limited to the last 30 days," "ask AI about these 200 notes" — with a one-tap upgrade right there. In-context prompts at the pain point beat any pricing-page redesign.

**5. Pricing and packaging come last.** Annual default, a visible "why upgrade" moment, maybe a time-limited trial of paid features for activated users. Only worth experimenting on once 1–4 are in place.

Where to focus first: spend the first week on the funnel split in step 1, because it tells you whether to invest in activation (step 2) or paywall placement (step 3) — and those are very different bets. If I had to commit before seeing data, I'd put money on activation being the bottleneck and the single highest-leverage move being a contextual paywall on **AI-over-your-notes or cross-device sync**, shown to already-retained users at the moment they hit the limit. That converts dependence you've already earned instead of trying to manufacture it at the pricing page.

One caveat worth naming: this assumes your problem is conversion mechanics. If retained, heavy users are churning *off the free tier entirely* rather than just declining to pay, that's a product-value gap and no paywall move will fix it — worth a quick look at whether your power users stay before you build any of this.

If you can share the actual funnel numbers (signups → activated → weekly-retained → paid), I can tell you which step is bleeding and skip the guesswork.

Low free-to-paid for a note app is almost always an activation-and-habit problem wearing a pricing costume, not a pricing problem. People pay for a tool they can't imagine losing — so the lever is getting them dependent, then putting the paywall exactly where a dependent user naturally hits a wall. Before you touch price, find out which of those two things is actually broken.

Here's the sketch, in the order the work should happen.

**1. Split the funnel before changing anything.** Separate two cohorts: users who never activated (signed up, wrote a few notes, drifted off) versus users who clearly retained (come back weekly, dozens of notes) but still won't pay. **4. Make the upgrade prompt contextual, not a settings page.** Trigger it at the moment of friction — "you've hit 3 devices," "search is limited to the last 30 days," "ask AI about these 200 notes" — with a one-tap upgrade right there. In-context prompts at the pain point beat any pricing-page redesign.

**5. Pricing and packaging come last.** Annual default, a visible "why upgrade" moment, maybe a time-limited trial of paid features for activated users. Only worth experimenting on once 1–4 are in place.

**Where to focus first — the #1 test to run, and what to hold.**

Run the funnel split in step 1 during week one — that's diagnosis, not the experiment. The single experiment I'd ship first is this:

> **Test #1 (run this before anything else): a contextual, in-app paywall on cross-device sync (or AI-over-your-notes), fired the moment an already-retained user hits the limit, with one-tap upgrade. Measure upgrade rate of retained users who see the prompt against a holdout that doesn't.**

It's first for three reasons: it targets dependence you've *already earned* rather than trying to manufacture it, it's a few days of work (a trigger plus a modal, not a rebuild), and its result tells you whether your problem is paywall *placement* or something deeper. Pick sync vs. AI by whichever your retained cohort already uses most — that's your sharpest existing dependence.

Explicitly **hold for later** (do *not* run these in parallel — they'll muddy the read on Test #1):
- All pricing and packaging changes (step 5) — price points, annual-vs-monthly, trial length. Don't touch price until the contextual-paywall test has reported.
- A free-tier feature shuffle (moving features in/out of free) — wait until Test #1 shows whether the gated dimension is the right one.
- Activation funnel rework (step 2) — *unless* the week-one split shows your retained pool is too small to power the test, in which case activation jumps the queue and becomes the new Test #1, because a paywall test needs a population of dependent users to fire at.

If I had to commit before seeing data, I'd bet activation is the underlying bottleneck — but the contextual-paywall test is still what I'd run first, because it's cheap, fast, and its outcome routes the rest of the roadmap.

One caveat worth naming: this assumes your problem is conversion mechanics. If retained, heavy users are churning *off the free tier entirely* rather than just declining to pay, that's a product-value gap and no paywall move will fix it — worth a quick look at whether your power users stay before you build any of this.

If you can share the actual funnel numbers (signups → activated → weekly-retained → paid), I can tell you which step is bleeding and skip the guesswork.
P:
Low free-to-paid on a note app is usually an activation-and-habit problem wearing a pricing costume. People pay for a tool they'd hate to lose, so the real lever is making them dependent and then placing the paywall exactly where a dependent user hits a wall. Before touching price, find out which of two things is actually broken: too few dependent users, or dependent users who won't pay. They have opposite fixes, and most teams fix the wrong one.

**Where to focus first:** spend week one on a funnel split, not a redesign. It's the one cheap move that tells you which bet to make. My prior, before seeing your data, is that most of your loss sits upstream of pricing — people who never got hooked — so the work lands on activation, not the paywall. But the split is what decides it, so run that first.

The sketch, in the order the work should happen:

**1. Split the funnel before changing anything.** Separate two cohorts: users who never activated (signed up, wrote a few notes, drifted off) and users who clearly retained (back every week, dozens of notes) but won't pay. The cut that matters: what share of signups are still creating notes in week two, and of those, what share ever sees — and declines — an upgrade prompt. If your retained users convert at a healthy clip (for freemium note tools, low-single-digit percent of *all* signups paying is normal) and you simply have few retained users, the problem is activation. If you have a pile of retained non-payers, it's genuinely the paywall. This is a week of analytics, and it changes everything downstream.

**2. If the leak is activation, fix that first — it's upstream of everything.** The goal is to reach "this is now where my notes live," fast: frictionless capture, importing the notes they already keep elsewhere, and sync working across phone and desktop on day one. The activation signal isn't a slick signup flow — it's coming back and writing on three separate days in the first week. No amount of paywall tuning matters if the habit never forms.

**3. Put the paywall on the dimension that compounds with dependence.** Free should be genuinely useful but capped on something a committed user inevitably outgrows: cross-device sync, search across your whole library, AI over your notes (summarize, ask questions, auto-organize), version history, attachment storage, export and sharing. The classic mistake is gating something people decide about on day one, when they don't care yet, instead of in month two, when leaving would cost them their notes.

**4. Make the upgrade prompt contextual, not a settings page.** Trigger it at the moment of friction — "you've hit your third device," "search only covers the last 30 days," "ask AI about all 200 of your notes" — with one-tap upgrade right there. An in-context prompt at the pain point beats any pricing-page redesign.

**5. Pricing and packaging come last.** Annual as the default, a clear "why upgrade" moment, maybe a time-limited trial of paid features for already-activated users. Only worth testing once 1–4 are in place.

How the two bets resolve once you have the split: if there are few retained users, your highest-leverage work is step 2, and a paywall redesign would just be polishing a leak you haven't plugged. If there's a pile of retained non-payers, the fastest win is step 4 — a contextual paywall on AI-over-your-notes or cross-device sync, shown to those users at the moment they hit the limit. That converts dependence you've already earned instead of trying to manufacture it at the pricing page.

One caveat worth naming: all of this assumes the problem is conversion mechanics. If your heavy, retained users are abandoning the free tier entirely rather than just declining to pay, that's a product-value gap no paywall move will fix — so glance at whether power users actually stay before building any of this.

Send me the real funnel — signups → activated → weekly-retained → paid — and I'll tell you which step is bleeding instead of guessing.
